elements.sent-2411-001: This corona comprises proteins that strongly bind to the NP (hard corona) and loosely bound proteins (soft corona) that dynamically exchange with the surrounding solution.
elements.sent-2411-002: While the kinetics of hard corona formation is relatively well understood, thanks to experiments and robust simulation models, the experimental characterization and simulation of the soft corona present a more significant challenge.
elements.sent-2411-003: Here, we review the current state of the art in soft corona characterization and introduce a novel open-source computational model to simulate its dynamic behavior, for which we provide the documentation.
elements.sent-2411-004: We focus on the case of transferrin (Tf) interacting with polystyrene NPs as an illustrative example, demonstrating how this model captures the complexities of the soft corona and offers deeper insights into its structure and behavior.
elements.sent-2411-005: We show that the soft corona is dominated by a glassy evolution that we relate to crowding effects.
elements.sent-2411-006: This work advances our understanding of the soft corona, bridging experimental limitations with improved simulation techniques.. \keyFont{.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Biomolecular Corona, Protein Adsorption Kinetics, Protein-Nanoparticle Interactions, Coarse-Grain modeling, Molecular Simulations, Transferrin.. \end{abstract.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Nanotechnology is a rapidly growing industry with emerging applications across various fields.
elements.sent-2411-009: Although nanostructures have been present in human life for a long time \cite{freestone2007lycurgus , the understanding and development of advanced nanomaterials are relatively new \cite{qiu2017scaling .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: In particular, there is an increasing interest in comprehending the behavior of nanoparticles (NPs) within biological systems \cite{dawson2021current .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The unique size, structure, and chemical properties of NPs introduce a wide range of new applications in many areas of research and technology, including therapeutics and diagnostics \cite{trinh2022nanoparticle .}
elements.sent-2411-012: When NPs are introduced into a biological environment, they quickly become coated by surrounding biomolecules, such as proteins, unless specifically designed not to do so \cite{Cedervall2007, Cedervall2007a, Lynch2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Research has shown that it is not the NP itself, but rather the biomolecules on its surface, that determine its interaction with living organisms.
elements.sent-2411-014: The layer of adsorbed proteins is known as the {\it protein corona and defines the biological identity of the NPs \cite{Lynch2009, Walczyk2010, Monopoli2012 .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Due to their size, NPs can distribute throughout organisms, reaching various cellular and organic compartments, and, in specific cases, they can even breach biological barriers, such as the blood-brain barrier \cite{Kreuter2002, Wagner2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In medicine, NPs can serve as therapeutic tools, enhancing previous techniques like drug delivery as they extend overall circulation time and the drug's efficacy \cite{Kumari2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, this characteristic also raises safety concerns regarding using NPs \cite{Oberdorster2005, Savolainen2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Therefore, given nanotechnology's immense potential, it is crucial to comprehend whether it poses a possible threat to organisms and the environment.
elements.sent-2411-019: Numerous experiments have been conducted to understand how blood plasma proteins bind to a nanoparticle.
elements.sent-2411-020: The findings indicate that proteins adhere to the surface of the NP, forming a corona around it, consisting of two different components \cite{Milani2012 : a {\it hard corona (HC) comprising tightly bound proteins in direct contact with the surface, and a more dynamic {\it soft corona (SC) that is in constant exchange with the protein solution (Fig.~\ref{figSnapshots ).}}}}

The relative concentration is $[\mathrm{Tf}]/[\mathrm{NP}]=2000$ and $[\mathrm{NP}]=1$ mg/ml. The panels are snapshots taken at simulation times increasing from left to right. a) Early-time formation of the hard corona. b) Intermediate-time partial formation of the hard and the soft corona. c) Large-time full formation of the hard and the soft corona.} \label{figSnapshots}
elements.sent-2411-021: Experiments show that the HC binds almost irreversibly to the NP, while the SC binds reversibly \cite{Lynch2009, Milani2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The HC is considered the most biologically relevant part as it interacts with cells and biological machinery via the receptors.
elements.sent-2411-023: On the other hand, the SC plays a role in mediating transient and dynamic interactions with biological systems \cite{garcia2021hard, bai2021vivo .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Characterizing the SC is challenging due to its transient nature and weak binding affinities, but recent advances help to understand its role.
elements.sent-2411-025: For example, using cryo-transmission electron microscopy (cryo-TEM) combined with synchrotron radiation circular dichroism (CD) allows real-time {\it in situ insights into the transient nature and dynamic evolution of the SC.}
elements.sent-2411-026: This approach reveals how weakly bound proteins within the SC undergo continuous exchange and reorganization \cite{sanchez2020situ .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Also, {\it in situ click-chemistry is used to map and identify the proteins within the SC, enabling precise tracking of protein-NP interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-028: This technique reveals that the dynamic and reversible nature of the SC proteins significantly influences NP uptake and interaction with cellular membranes \cite{mohammad2020mapping .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Furthermore, the analysis of the protein corona on gold NPs by Liquid Chromatography-Tandem Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) and Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA) has found that only 27\% of the adsorbed proteins were functional for binding \cite{zhang2020analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-030: This indicates that the corona, rather than a simple monolayer, is an assembly of layers comprising a foundation layer (i.e., the HC) and an intermediate corona (IC) plus a binding layer, with the last two usually identified as SC.
elements.sent-2411-031: Recent studies have combined different experimental methods to characterize MoS$_2$ nanomaterials {\it in vivo and have demonstrated that the SC mediates in the biodistribution, transformation, and bioavailability of nanoparticles, thus impacting liver metabolism and enzyme activity \cite{cao2021molybdenum .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Additionally, a "fishing" method has been developed using bio-layer interferometry and LC-MS/MS to monitor the dynamic formation and evolution of the protein corona on chiral Cu$_2$S nanoparticles \cite{baimanov2022situ .}
elements.sent-2411-033: This technique allows for real-time tracking and detailed characterization of the SC.
elements.sent-2411-034: To understand how different layers form on the surface of NPs, particularly the SC, it is essential to characterize the kinetics of protein adsorption into the corona.
elements.sent-2411-035: The rate at which proteins are adsorbed and the protein corona formation depend greatly on protein concentrations and affinities.
elements.sent-2411-036: Competition and cooperation between different types of proteins are crucial mechanisms in understanding kinetic processes, such as the Vroman effect \cite{Vroman1969, Vroman1986a, LeDuc1995, Vilaseca2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Only biomolecules that reside in the protein corona for longer than the characteristic timescale of a biological phenomenon are likely to be relevant to the process.
elements.sent-2411-038: Experiments have shown how tissues, organs, and other biological systems respond depending on how long biomolecules stay at the NP surface \cite{Tran2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that proteins with a long residence time near the surface are the ones that give the NP its biological identity.
elements.sent-2411-040: On the other hand, the proteins that attach to the NP only temporarily, depending on the current environment, may not be relevant.
elements.sent-2411-041: These two different timescales suggest a memory effect on the NP.
elements.sent-2411-042: Exposing the NP to a new environment with different biomolecules could result in a partially new corona composition.
elements.sent-2411-043: Biomolecules that have not been replaced in the protein corona serve as a record of previous environments visited by the NP.
elements.sent-2411-044: This was studied in an earlier work, where it was demonstrated, with experiments and simulations, the memory effect on silica NPs suspended in solution with Human Serum Albumin (HSA) and Transferrin (Tf) using simulations and experiments \cite{Vilanova2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Numerical simulations can provide valuable insights into the multilayer adsorption of proteins in the corona.
elements.sent-2411-046: However, they represent a challenge when compared with the experiments.
elements.sent-2411-047: Indeed, experimental techniques like Fluorescence Correlation Spectroscopy (FCS), used to quantify protein adsorption, typically involve very low concentrations of NPs \cite{Rusu2004, Milani2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: At the same time, in biological cases of interest, protein concentrations are often very high, as in the case of SDS-PAGE (Sodium Dodecyl Sulfate-Polyacrylamide Gel Electrophoresis) experiments \cite{Lundqvist2011, Pitek2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: These conditions correspond to the worst-case scenario in molecular simulation.
elements.sent-2411-050: Indeed, low NP concentrations require extensive systems, eventually made of a large simulation box containing only a single NP.
elements.sent-2411-051: However, the need for high protein concentrations leads to an exponential increase in the number of proteins, making it demanding to simulate the processes.
elements.sent-2411-052: Furthermore, the problem becomes more challenging when simulation time scales need to match actual experiments' time frames, typically in minutes or even hours.
elements.sent-2411-053: To achieve significant results within a specific timescale, it is crucial to select an appropriate simulation scheme, which is determined by the integration time step and the level of coarse-graining used in the simulation technique.
elements.sent-2411-054: Indeed, a full-atom description of thousands of interacting proteins is out of the present reach of even the most powerful computer clusters.
elements.sent-2411-055: Therefore, researchers resort to coarse-grained approaches that reduce the degrees of freedom involved in the biological systems at the nanoscale and simplify the description of the relevant interparticle interactions.
elements.sent-2411-056: Meaningful information can still be obtained using a minimal system description focusing on the most appropriate degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-057: While a general description of the solvent is essential for many biological processes \cite{polym13010156, Fauli:2023aa , the effects of hydration on protein interactions can be accounted for using effective potentials, especially when the model's transferability at different thermodynamic conditions is not crucial.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Additionally, employing the Langevin integration scheme for the equations of motion allows for the simulation of the thermal energy contribution from water \cite{Vilanova2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: In this simplified description, proteins are the primary focus, and their number influences the simulation dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-060: Better computational performances can optimize numerical simulations, and parallelization is a straightforward way to achieve this goal.
elements.sent-2411-061: Over the past decade, Graphical Processing Units (GPUs) have emerged as a cost-effective and computationally efficient choice for implementing molecular simulations \cite{Walters2008, Anderson2008, Harvey2009, Trott2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-062: Hence, the codes we describe and use in the following are developed within the CUDA$^{\textsuperscript{\textregistered }$ framework, which is compatible with most NVIDIA$^{\textsuperscript{\textregistered }$ GPUs.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Specifically, we developed BUBBLES ("BUBBLES is a User-friendly Bundle for Bio-nano Large-scale Efficient Simulations"), a suite of tools designed for simulating the interactions and kinetics of NPs in biological environments, modeled as aqueous solutions containing proteins \cite{Bubbles2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Here, as a case study, we use BUBBLE to analyze the SC kinetics of Tf adsorbing onto polystyrene (PSCOOH) NPs.
elements.sent-2411-065: Polystyrene is a common polymer used in the production of plastic, such as in packaging materials, food containers, and disposable cups.
elements.sent-2411-066: These NPs have a low density and high surface area-to-volume ratio, making them useful in applications like electronics or biomedical research, despite health concerns arising \cite{kik2020polystyrene .}
elements.sent-2411-067: On the other hand, Tf is one of the most abundant proteins in human plasma \cite{schenk2008high .}
elements.sent-2411-068: It is a glycoprotein with a molecular weight of around 80 kDa, composed of two subunits of equal size joined by a disulfide bridge.
elements.sent-2411-069: Each subunit has a single site for binding iron \cite{gomme2005transferrin .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Iron atoms are absorbed in the intestine, bound to Tf in the plasma, and then transported to storage and utilization sites such as the bone marrow and the liver \cite{gkouvatsos2012regulation .}
elements.sent-2411-071: We aim to describe the results observed in experiments involving polystyrene NPs in solutions containing Tf \cite{Milani2012 and to predict by numerical simulations of our coarse-grained model the short-time dynamics of the SC formation.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Next, to validate the model’s applicability in protein-rich environments, we use Differential Centrifugal Sedimentation (DCS) to assess NPs size distribution after exposure to Tf.
elements.sent-2411-073: When simulating biological systems interacting with nanoscale objects using atomistic simulations, handling many proteins for long simulation time scales is prohibitive.
elements.sent-2411-074: Additionally, a significant amount of resources are needed to simulate the solvent.
elements.sent-2411-075: To address this, we developed a coarse-grain approach that provides a simplified system description.
elements.sent-2411-076: This method retains all the essential molecular details, making it suitable for studying bio-nano interactions at the mesoscale.
elements.sent-2411-077: Our approach is based on an implicit solvent description, which uses phenomenological parameters and significantly reduces the computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-078: However, the strong approximations in the model make it not transferable to different thermodynamic conditions or protein-NP combinations.

elements.sent-2411-222: All these observations consistently associate the innermost layer with the HC and the two outermost layers with the SC, validating the conclusions drawn from the RDF analysis.

elements.sent-2411-232: To better characterize the soft corona dynamics, we analyze the autocorrelation function for the proteins populating the SC$_1$ and SC$_2$ layers, \begin{equation}C_i(t) \equiv \frac{\langle N_{{Ads}, i}(t_0) N_{{Ads}, i}(t_0 + t) \rangle - \langle N_{{Ads}, i}(t_0) \rangle \langle N_{{Ads}, i}(t_0 + t) \rangle}{\langle N_{{Ads}, i}(t_0)^2 \rangle - \langle N_{{Ads}, i}(t_0) \rangle^2} \label{corr}\end{equation} where $N_{{Ads , i (t)$ is the time-dependent number of Tf proteins within the layer SC$_i$ with $i=1$, 2, and the averages are taken over the initial time $t_0$ larger than the characteristic time needed for the layers to stabilize its population, i.e., $\simeq 2$ sec for [Tf]/[NP]= 1500 (Fig.~\ref{figAdsorption b, c).}}}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}C_i(t) \equiv \frac{\langle N_{{Ads}, i}(t_0) N_{{Ads}, i}(t_0 + t) \rangle - \langle N_{{Ads}, i}(t_0) \rangle \langle N_{{Ads}, i}(t_0 + t) \rangle}{\langle N_{{Ads}, i}(t_0)^2 \rangle - \langle N_{{Ads}, i}(t_0) \rangle^2} \label{corr}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-233: The two SC layers exhibit distinct behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-234: The $C_2(t)$ for the outer layer SC$_{2}$ demonstrates a decay pattern that departs from exponential, appearing closer to a power law across two decades.
elements.sent-2411-235: Exponential behavior would be anticipated if the system had achieved equilibrium, resulting in decorrelated population fluctuations of SC$_{2}$ over a characteristic time scale.
elements.sent-2411-236: A power-law decay suggests that such a time scale does not exist, indicating that the fluctuations remain correlated over durations exceeding our observation window of 0.1 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-237: Conversely, the inner layer of the soft corona, SC$_{1}$, begins with a rapid decay, transitioning into a plateau that persists for nearly one decade.
elements.sent-2411-238: Over longer timeframes, $C_1(t)$ departs from the plateau, potentially adopting a power-law decay.
elements.sent-2411-239: This non-exponential behavior in the correlation function is characteristic of glassy systems, where dynamic progress is confined within local free-energy minima.
elements.sent-2411-240: The greater the system's distance from equilibrium, the higher the plateau in the autocorrelation function \cite{Gotze:1992fk, Kumar2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-241: The relaxation behavior in the SC's inner layer implies that the corona's dynamic evolution in this volume is affected by factors such as crowding from other proteins within the corona.
elements.sent-2411-242: These factors can restrict protein mobility, leading to the development of dynamically {\it frozen protein clusters or aggregates, which contribute to the relaxation behavior observed.}

elements.sent-2411-261: When NPs come into contact with biological fluids, biomolecules adhere to their surfaces, forming a corona composed of multiple layers that influence how these NPs interact with cells and their biological effects.
elements.sent-2411-262: In protein-rich environments, such as blood or serum, strong protein-NP interactions form a hard corona.
elements.sent-2411-263: In contrast, weaker protein-protein and protein-NP interactions give rise to a soft corona.
elements.sent-2411-264: The hard corona is characterized by proteins that adsorb irreversibly onto the NP, whereas the soft corona consists of a fluid layer of loosely bound proteins associated with the NP.
elements.sent-2411-265: Understanding the dynamics of each component of the protein corona is essential for various biological applications of nanotechnology \cite{Sharma2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-266: However, measuring the soft corona poses significant experimental challenges \cite{Guo:2024aa .}
elements.sent-2411-267: These modifications risk altering protein binding capacities, thereby complicating accurate measurements.
elements.sent-2411-268: Additionally, excessive centrifugal force during separation may result in the loss of protein corona components, further complicating accurate detection.
elements.sent-2411-269: Therefore, the composition of the protein corona has traditionally been examined, either {\it in vivo or {\it in vitro , through static incubation methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-270: Nevertheless, this approach limits our ability to explore the dynamics of the corona components over biologically relevant timescales.
elements.sent-2411-271: Here, we present new findings for a computational method that enables us to integrate experimental results and comprehensively describe the dynamics of both the hard and soft corona.
elements.sent-2411-272: We extend the modeling and computational approach we previously introduced, incorporating the study of the soft corona into analyzing the short- and long-term kinetics of the hard corona formed by different proteins competing for the NP surface \cite{Vilanova2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-273: The method employs a coarse-grained description of globular proteins interacting with the NP through colloid-like effective potential energies.
elements.sent-2411-274: This approach allows for molecular simulations over time scales on the order of seconds within the framework of Langevin dynamics once a few phenomenological parameters are extracted from preliminary experiments with monocomponent protein solutions.
elements.sent-2411-275: These simulation predictions can then be extended to any time scale and validated against laboratory results, utilizing a non-Langmuir adsorption theory introduced in Ref.~\cite{Vilanova2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-276: The extension introduced here incorporates a three-body interaction between proteins and the NP, mimicking the denaturation effect that the nanosurface can induce on proteins \cite{park2020protein .}
elements.sent-2411-277: We assume that this effect exponentially decays as the distance between the NP’s surface and any interacting proteins increases.
elements.sent-2411-278: The alignment of our simulation results with experimental data validates our model {\it a posteriori .}
elements.sent-2411-279: As a case study, we consider the experimental conditions described in Ref.~\cite{Milani2012 , which evaluated the formation of the hard and soft corona by Tf proteins in suspension with polystyrene NPs.}
elements.sent-2411-280: The experimental setup presents challenges due to the highly diluted concentrations of NPs and proteins, necessitating macroscopically large systems that are computationally prohibitive to simulate.
elements.sent-2411-281: To address this, we develop a scaling strategy to replicate the overall experimental behavior in smaller systems with higher concentrations and effective parameters.
elements.sent-2411-282: Using as an example the parameter describing the exponential decay of the three-body protein-protein-NP interaction, we show how this strategy allows us to extrapolate the quantity of interest in the limit of the experimental conditions.
elements.sent-2411-283: After establishing the model, we used it to investigate the structure and kinetics of the corona.
elements.sent-2411-284: Our structural analysis reveals that the corona is composed of three distinct layers.
elements.sent-2411-285: The layer closest to the NP’s surface is the sharpest, while the outermost layer is the broadest.
elements.sent-2411-286: The first and second layers become populated at low and moderate protein concentrations, whereas the third layer forms only at the highest concentrations.
elements.sent-2411-287: The protein dynamics in each layer reveals that the first layer, which is in direct contact with the NP, contains proteins tightly bound to the surface.
elements.sent-2411-288: These proteins adsorb irreversibly over the simulation time scales, forming the hard corona.
elements.sent-2411-289: In contrast, the proteins in the second and third layers are reversibly bound to the corona, constituting the soft corona.
elements.sent-2411-290: Our study shows that the first (HC) and second (SC$_1$) layers reach saturation at the protein concentrations examined and that, overall, the entire corona needs approximately 2 seconds to reach an apparent stable number of adsorbed proteins at the highest [Tf]/[NP] considered here.
elements.sent-2411-291: Furthermore, the time required to get 90\% saturation within a given layer decreases as protein concentration increases, with the HC approaching a characteristic value of about 0.2 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-292: This trend is similar for the soft corona SC$_1$ layer, but the characteristic time to reach 90\% saturation is about twice that for the HC (Fig.\ref{tau ).}
elements.sent-2411-293: Although the protein concentrations are insufficient to saturate the third layer (SC$_2$), a similar trend is observed for the outermost part of the soft corona, with saturation times for SC$_2$ that are two or three times larger than SC$_1$.
elements.sent-2411-294: Overall, the time required to stabilize a layer at a given relative concentration [Tf]/[NP] increases from the hard corona to the outermost layer of the soft corona, ranging from approximately 0.2 seconds to around 1.0 seconds at the highest concentration considered in this study.

The time $\tau$ is defined as the time needed to saturate 90\% of the corresponding layer in the corona (black circles for HC, red squares for SC$_1$). Saturation times are estimated based on data in Fig.~\ref{figAdsorption}.} \label{tau}
elements.sent-2411-295: The in-depth analysis of the dynamics of the soft corona reveals that SC$_1$ displays a glassy behavior.
elements.sent-2411-296: Its density autocorrelation function has a nonexponential decay and develops a plateau at intermediate times.
elements.sent-2411-297: We understand this behavior as a consequence of the crowding of the inner layer caused by the outer one.
elements.sent-2411-298: Hence, although the time $\tau$ needed to reach 90\% of its saturation for the soft corona is on the order of seconds, the corona could evolve over longer times as in glassy systems.
elements.sent-2411-299: We expect that at higher biomolecular concentrations, such as those in blood, this slowing down could become relevant for biological processes and should be taken into account when analyzing the effects of the protein soft-corona in the NP-cell interaction over time.
elements.sent-2411-300: We validate our model predictions by directly estimating, under similar experimental conditions, the number of Tf proteins adsorbed onto 100 nm PSCOOH polystyrene NPs.
elements.sent-2411-301: Despite the differences, mainly related to the nominal size of the NPs, our experiments show that the computational model can predict within a 10\% error the number of adsorbed proteins at the different relative concentrations [Tf]/[NP] considered here.
elements.sent-2411-302: We observe that the model is, overall, predicting a faster-increasing number of proteins adsorbed with the increase of the [Tf]/[NP] ratio relative to the experiments.
elements.sent-2411-303: This difference is possibly due to the agglomeration of the NPs observed in the experiment at increasing [Tf]/[NP].
elements.sent-2411-304: The clustering of more than one NP-corona complex could 1) decrease the surface area available for protein binding and 2) lead to an experimental underestimation in the number of adsorbed Tf due to the density value used for Tf.
elements.sent-2411-305: Lastly, the computational model does not consider Tf structural changes upon adsorption.
elements.sent-2411-306: Instead, recent findings from cryo-TEM corona show a uniform corona layer rather than a packing of globular proteins, suggesting structural deformation of the protein structure \cite{sheibani2021nanoscale .}
elements.sent-2411-307: This deformation could correspond to the protein flattening out, which would reduce the number of adsorbed Tf.
elements.sent-2411-308: The overall conclusion is that the close correlation between the experimental and computational models in the present case study demonstrates satisfactory performance in protein-rich environments, indicating the applicability of our approach to biologically relevant conditions.
elements.sent-2411-309: For this reason, to benefit the scientific community, we provide an open-source interactive tutorial with all the steps required to perform the simulations, defining and implementing a buffer of molecules capable of controlling the concentration of proteins in the vicinity of the NP \cite{Bubbles2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-310: Future research in this field of NP-corona interactions with biological systems should expand beyond the limited scope of studying the competition among just a few types of proteins.
elements.sent-2411-311: Biological media, such as blood plasma, are incredibly complex and contain over 3,700 identified proteins, leading to a highly competitive environment for the formation of the corona.
elements.sent-2411-312: This phenomenon, known as the Vroman effect, highlights the dynamic nature of protein adsorption, where proteins with higher mobility and lower affinity initially occupy the NP surface, only to be replaced by proteins with higher affinity over time.
elements.sent-2411-313: In addition to proteins, several other molecules in whole blood can contribute to the formation of the NP corona \cite{Lundqvist0175871 , including lipids, carbohydrates, nucleic acids, metabolites, complement factors, and antibodies \cite{D4EN00488D .}}
elements.sent-2411-314: For example, lipids such as cholesterol and phospholipids, glycans—which are carbohydrate molecules that play a role in cell recognition and signaling—or small metabolites like glucose, hormones, and vitamins can adsorb onto NPs, altering their surface properties and influencing their interactions with cells \cite{D0NA00863J .}
elements.sent-2411-315: On the other hand, circulating DNA is present in the blood of all individuals \cite{Vaart1448.022 , and its concentration increases in cancer patients \cite{Jahr2001DNAFI .}}
elements.sent-2411-316: Furthermore, depending on the state of different diseases, blood plasma also contains a variety of RNA types, including mRNAs, noncoding RNAs, and fragments of rRNAs, snoRNAs, and miRNAs \cite{Savelyeva7404912, Semenov1448.030 , suggesting that the NP-corona composition also depends on the health condition of the host.}
elements.sent-2411-317: Moreover, complement factors that are part of the immune system and immunoglobulins or antibodies, which are glycoproteins produced by plasma cells, can mark the NP for clearance by immune cells \cite{D0NA00863J .}
elements.sent-2411-318: Therefore, it will be critical in the future to understand how all these components collectively form the {\it biomolecular hard and soft corona, which significantly impacts the biological identity and fate of NPs in the body \cite{D4EN00488D .}}
elements.sent-2411-319: To gain a comprehensive understanding of NP behavior in biological environments, it will be crucial to investigate how these biomolecules operate under healthy or disease conditions.
elements.sent-2411-320: This approach will provide insights into real-world scenarios and how stressful conditions influence the NPs' biological identity and subsequent interactions with cells and tissues.
elements.sent-2411-321: Moreover, the study of the dynamics and aging behavior of the biomolecular corona will be essential.
elements.sent-2411-322: As discussed here, the corona's composition and structure can change over time, exhibiting glassy behavior characterized by slow dynamics and structural rearrangements.
elements.sent-2411-323: Understanding these processes is vital for predicting the fate and stability of NPs in biological environments.
elements.sent-2411-324: The glassy state of the corona may play a significant role in determining the long-term interactions of NPs with biological systems, impacting their efficacy and safety in medical applications.
elements.sent-2411-325: Considering the corona's glassy states is particularly important for the development of stable pharmaceuticals.
elements.sent-2411-326: Ensuring that the corona remains properly formulated when NPs are immersed in biological fluids is crucial for maintaining their intended function and avoiding unintended side effects.
elements.sent-2411-327: This knowledge can guide the design of NP-based drug delivery systems, enhancing their stability and performance in the complex biological milieu.
elements.sent-2411-328: From a computational perspective, the scientific community will need to develop new approaches to account for the interaction of NP-corona complexes with cells, particularly focusing on their interaction with cell membranes.

elements.sent-2411-338: In summary, future studies should adopt a holistic approach that considers the vast array of molecules present in biological media and the competitive interactions that occur on NP surfaces and within the soft corona.
elements.sent-2411-339: Additionally, a deeper understanding of the dynamic and aging behaviors of the biomolecular corona, as well as the water-mediated interaction with the cell membrane, will be instrumental in advancing NP applications in medicine, leading to the development of more effective and safer nanotherapeutics.
